evidence: All success responses include 'status': 'ok' via direct put or via sendJsonResponse
All error responses return '{"error": "..."}' via HttpApiUtils.sendError with consistent HTTP codes (400, 413, 500)
explore and explore-multi both echo request parameters in a 'parameters' sub-map; compare does not include 'relation_type' in response (intentional, cross-relational)
All count fields follow the '{name}_count' suffix convention: seed_collocates_count, discovered_nouns_count, adjectives_count, edges_count

ConcordanceHandlers /api/concordance/examples response uses keys 'seed'/'collocate' matching HTTP param names; /api/semantic-field/examples response uses 'adjective'/'noun' matching its HTTP param names — two example endpoints use different vocabulary for analogous subject/collocate roles
/api/semantic-field/compare response omits 'relation_type' key present in explore and explore-multi responses
BCQL /api/bcql response echoes 'top' (previously 'top_requested') matching the request parameter name — consistent

strengths: status: 'ok' / error: '...' envelope is applied uniformly across all endpoints — a consumer can write a single response checker
Explore endpoints consistently echo request parameters in a parameters sub-map, enabling request/response correlation without state
All count fields use a consistent {name}_count suffix — a consumer can predict what to look for
CORS headers are applied by every send method — consumers never receive a response without CORS headers
HTTP method enforcement (GET for exploration/sketch, POST for bcql/radial) is consistent and returns 405 with Allow header for mismatches
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Two localised vocabulary inconsistencies at the boundary between similar-purpose endpoints — explore and compare endpoints use different vocabularies for the 'subject' concept (seed vs noun, relation_type presence), creating a small but real learning curve when a consumer uses both.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The core envelope (status, error, parameters echo, count fields) is uniformly applied — a developer can predict the shape of any response from reading one example. The two issues are real but narrow: the relation_type omission in compare is cross-relational and intentional (noted in the Javadoc), and the adjective/noun vs seed/collocate vocabulary split affects only the two example endpoints. Neither causes functional breakage but both require a consumer to read two endpoint specs rather than inferring one from the other. This fits 83 — solid with recurring minor irregularities.

issues.summary: /api/concordance/examples and /api/semantic-field/examples use different keys for equivalent noun/adjective roles
evidence: /api/concordance/examples response: 'response.put("seed", req.noun())' and 'response.put("collocate", req.adjective())' — HTTP params are seed/collocate
/api/semantic-field/examples response: 'response.put("adjective", adjective)' and 'response.put("noun", noun)' — HTTP params are adjective/noun
Both endpoints return concordance examples for an adjective-noun pair; a frontend developer consuming both must map seed→noun, collocate→adjective and vice versa
issues.suggestion: Standardise on 'noun'/'adjective' across both example endpoints. In ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples change: 'response.put("seed", req.noun())' → 'response.put("noun", req.noun())' and 'response.put("collocate", req.adjective())' → 'response.put("adjective", req.adjective())'. The HTTP params (seed/collocate) can remain as they are — only the response body keys need to change. Update the Javadoc comment in ConcordanceHandlers to reflect the new response keys.

issues.summary: /api/semantic-field/compare response omits relation_type present in sibling explore endpoints without documenting the omission in the response contract
evidence: explore and explore-multi responses include 'response.put("relation_type", relationType)' at the top level
compare response (populateComparisonResponse) does not include 'relation_type' — intentional because it is cross-relational
The Javadoc of handleSemanticFieldComparison explains why there is no relation parameter, but does not mention that the response omits relation_type
A frontend developer seeing relation_type in explore responses and building a unified response parser would need to add a special case for compare
issues.suggestion: Add a note to the response contract in the handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc: '@apiNote The response intentionally omits {@code relation_type} because comparison aggregates across all loaded relations rather than filtering to one.' Alternatively, include 'relation_type': 'cross_relational' in the compare response body to give consumers a uniform key with a sentinel value. The documentation fix is lower-risk; the sentinel value approach is more consumer-friendly.

root_causes: ConcordanceHandlers and ExplorationHandlers were developed independently; no shared response vocabulary was enforced
Compare endpoint intentionally omits relation_type but the omission was not documented in the response contract
